questions.question: Explain the three properties of CAP Theorem: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
keyPoints: Consistency: All nodes see the same data at the same time
Availability: Every request receives a non-error response
Partition Tolerance: System continues to function despite network partitions

questions.question: What does CAP Theorem state about distributed systems? Why is it impossible to achieve all three properties simultaneously?
keyPoints: CAP Theorem states only 2 of 3 properties can be guaranteed at any time
During network partition, must choose between consistency and availability
Network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems

questions.question: Differentiate between CP systems and AP systems with one example of each.
keyPoints: CP systems sacrifice availability to maintain consistency (e.g., MongoDB, HBase)
AP systems sacrifice consistency to maintain availability (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB)
During partition, CP becomes unavailable while AP may serve stale data

questions.question: What is eventual consistency? How does it differ from strong consistency?
keyPoints: Eventual consistency allows temporary inconsistencies but guarantees convergence
Strong consistency ensures all nodes have identical data immediately after writes
Eventual consistency prioritizes availability and performance

questions.question: Explain the PACELC model as an extension to CAP Theorem.
keyPoints: PACELC adds latency consideration when there is no partition
P-A/E-C: If Partition, choose Availability or Consistency; Else choose Latency or Consistency
Addresses limitations of CAP for normal operation scenarios

questions.question: Why are traditional RDBMS considered CA systems? What happens to them during network partitions?
keyPoints: RDBMS prioritize Consistency and Availability
They cannot tolerate network partitions
During partition, they may become unavailable to maintain consistency

questions.question: Give two examples each of CP, AP, and CA systems and explain their typical use cases.
keyPoints: CP: MongoDB, HBase (banking, inventory systems requiring accuracy)
AP: Cassandra, DynamoDB (social media, caching where availability matters)
CA: MySQL, PostgreSQL (single-node applications)

questions.question: Discuss the practical implications of CAP Theorem for designing modern distributed applications. How should developers choose between CP and AP architectures?
keyPoints: Understand application requirements: data accuracy vs. uptime
CP suitable for applications where consistency is critical (financial, transactional)
AP suitable for applications where availability is critical (content delivery, messaging)
Consider trade-offs: CP may lose customers during outages, AP may serve incorrect data
Hybrid approaches and configurable systems exist

questions.question: Explain what happens when a network partition occurs in a distributed system. How do systems decide whether to choose consistency or availability? What factors influence this decision?
keyPoints: Network partition is a communication breakdown between nodes
CP: reject requests to prevent inconsistent reads
AP: serve requests from any available node, may return stale data
Decision factors: business requirements, data criticality, user expectations
Some systems allow configurable consistency levels

questions.question: Compare and contrast MongoDB and Apache Cassandra in terms of their CAP Theorem classification. Discuss scenarios where each would be more appropriate.
keyPoints: MongoDB: CP system, strong consistency, primary-secondary replication
Cassandra: AP system, eventual consistency, multi-master replication
MongoDB suitable for: applications requiring ACID guarantees, complex queries
Cassandra suitable for: high write throughput, geographically distributed data
MongoDB trade-off: potential unavailability during partition
Cassandra trade-off: possible stale reads

questions.question: Critically analyze whether the CAP Theorem is still relevant in modern cloud-native applications. Discuss any criticisms or extensions to the original theorem.
keyPoints: CAP Theorem oversimplifies trade-offs (latency vs consistency not considered)
PACELC extends CAP for normal operation scenarios
Modern systems often allow tunable consistency
Not all partitions are equal - partial availability can be achieved
CAP describes extreme points, not continuous spectrum
